en.extract:
The  Malabar whistling thrush (Myophonus horsfieldii) is a whistling thrush in the family Muscicapidae. The bird has been called whistling schoolboy for the whistling calls that they make at dawn that have a very human quality. The species is a resident in the Western Ghats and associated hills of peninsular India including central India and parts of the Eastern Ghats.


Taxonomy and systematics
This is a monotypic taxon previously considered conspecific with Taiwan whistling thrush.


Description and biology
It is a large thrush measuring about 25-30 cm and weighs about 101-130 g. The male has a blackish upper body with shiny metallic patches of blue on the forehead and shoulders, and glossy royal-blue scaling on back, scapulars and mid-breast to belly.  The bill and legs are black. The female is very similar, but with weaker scaling below. Juveniles are black, with glossy blue shoulder patch and wing edgings. The blue becomes visible only in oblique lighting and is due to ultraviolet reflectance, a shared attribute with other whistling thrushes.


Distribution and habitat

Malabar whistling thrushes are usually found in dark undergrowth in dense riverine forest. They typically forage in the margins, beds and adjacent ground of rocky hill streams and rivers in forest, secondary growth and plantations from foothills up to 2200 m above sea level but reach the plains in the rainy season. The species is found all along the Western Ghats south of the Surat Dangs. They are also found along the Satpura range to Chhattisgarh, northwestern Orissa (Surguja and Simlipal National Park), and locally in the Eastern Ghats. Populations are not migratory but are known to disperse widely in winter. An individual that was ringed in Mahabaleshwar in the summer of 1972 was recovered in the winter of 1976 in Sampaje, Coorg. Although historically recorded twice from Mount Abu, more recent surveys have not recorded the bird or suitable habitat in that location.


Behaviour and ecology
They are usually seen singly or in pairs. Wherever there is suitable habitat, they are often found close to human habitation. The male sings its varied and melodious whistling song from trees during summer. They may sing for a long time around dawn but at other times of the day they often utter sharp single or two note high-pitched whistles. They were once popular as cage birds, with the ability to learn entire tunes. They bathe frequently in water usually in the mornings and evenings but also at midday during hot weather.


Food and feeding
The species is omnivorous, feeding primarily on insects, snails, worms, crabs and small frogs, as well as drupes, and wind-fallen figs and berries. Occasionally they have been recorded eating small snakes and rare records of eating longer snakes such as the Rat snake have been reported. 


Breeding

The birds breed from March to December and begin with the arrival of the monsoons. Courtship involves chasing flights and calling. They usually nest in a cavity on a stream bank but will sometimes make use of nearby buildings. In a study of nest-site selection in the Silent Valley area, a total of 21 nests were found mostly on rocks along the edge of streams and one each in a tree hole and inside an abandoned building. The nest is a cup made of moss, bamboo roots and grass, with a broad base and tapering towards the top. The base of the nest appeared to be cemented to the rock with mud. Mean nest height was 14.8 cm and depth 7.4 cm. The mean outer width and inner width were 21.5 cm and 13.1 cm respectively. Mean height from the ground was 125.8 cm. Most nest sites were about 6 m from water with 60% rock cover. The nests were mostly fully concealed and nesting success was directly related to it. The birds show high site fidelity; occupying and nesting near their previous season's nests. The clutch consists of 2 to 4 eggs. The eggs are pale salmon pink with speckling. The eggs are incubated for about 16 or 17 days by both the male and female.


References


Other sources
Harish, B T (1977) The Malabar Whistling Thrush. Newsletter for Birdwatchers ., India. 17(11):8.
Thakker, P S (1980) Malabar Whistling Thrush and Chestnutheaded Bee-eater. Newsletter for Birdwatchers ., India. 20(11), 3–4.
Navarro, A (1976) The Whistling Thrush – the harbinger of the monsoon. Newsletter for Birdwatchers . 16(11):5–7

The ''' Malabar whistling thrush''' ('''''Myophonus horsfieldii''''') is a [[whistling thrush]] in the family [[Old World flycatcher|''Muscicapidae'']]. The bird has been called ''whistling schoolboy'' for the whistling calls that they make at dawn that have a very human quality.<ref name="mccann" /> The species is a resident in the [[Western Ghats]] and associated hills of peninsular [[India]] including central India and parts of the [[Eastern Ghats]].

== Taxonomy and systematics ==
This is a [[monotypic taxon]] previously considered conspecific with [[Taiwan whistling thrush]].<ref name=":0" />

==Description and biology==
It is a large thrush measuring about 25-30 cm<ref name=":0">{{Citation|last1=Collar|first1=Nigel|title=Malabar Whistling-Thrush (Myophonus horsfieldii)|date=2020-03-04|url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/mawthr1/1.0/introduction|work=Birds of the World|editor-last=Billerman|editor-first=Shawn M.|publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology|language=en|doi=10.2173/bow.mawthr1.01|access-date=2022-02-01|last2=Bonan|first2=Arnau|editor2-last=Keeney|editor2-first=Brooke K.|editor3-last=Rodewald|editor3-first=Paul G.|editor4-last=Schulenberg|editor4-first=Thomas S.|url-access=subscription}}</ref> and weighs about 101-130 g. The male has a blackish upper body with shiny metallic patches of blue on the forehead and shoulders, and glossy royal-blue scaling on back, scapulars and mid-breast to belly.  The bill and legs are black. The female is very similar, but with weaker scaling below. Juveniles are black, with glossy blue shoulder patch and wing edgings. The blue becomes visible only in oblique lighting and is due to [[ultraviolet]] [[reflectance]], a shared attribute with other [[Whistling thrush|whistling thrushes]].<ref name=":0" /><ref name=pcr>{{cite book|author1=Rasmussen PC  |author2=Anderton, JC  |name-list-style=amp | year=2005| title = Birds of South Asia: The Ripley Guide. Volume 2|publisher=Smithsonian Institution & Lynx Edicions| page=371}}</ref><ref name=hbk>{{cite book|author1=Ali, S  |author2=Ripley, S D  |name-list-style=amp | year=1998 | title= Handbook of the birds of India and Pakistan|pages=79–81| publisher=Oxford University Press|volume=9 |edition=2nd}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|date=1996-07-22|title=Bright ultraviolet colouration in the Asian whistling-thrushes ( Myiophonus spp .)|url=https://royalsocietypublishing.org/doi/10.1098/rspb.1996.0124|journal=Proceedings of the Royal Society of London. Series B: Biological Sciences|language=en|volume=263|issue=1372|pages=843–848|doi=10.1098/rspb.1996.0124|issn=0962-8452|url-access=subscription}}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
[[File:Malabar Whistling Thrush by Shantanu Kuveskar.jpg|thumb|In Mangaon, Raigad, Maharashtra]]
Malabar whistling thrushes are usually found in dark undergrowth in dense riverine forest.<ref name="pcr" /> They typically forage in the margins, beds and adjacent ground of rocky hill streams and rivers in forest, secondary growth and plantations from foothills up to 2200 m above sea level but reach the plains in the rainy season.<ref name=":0" /> The species is found all along the Western Ghats south of the Surat [[Dangs (Vidhan Sabha constituency)|Dangs]]. They are also found along the [[Satpura Range|Satpura range]] to Chhattisgarh,<ref>Vishwakarma, Anurag, Ravi Naidu2 P. Vijay Sitaram Raju, and Saurabh Singh. "Range extension of Malabar Whistling Thrush (Myophonus horsfieldii) in Chhattisgarh." ''Newsletter for Birdwatchers'' 59.6 (2019): 73.</ref> northwestern Orissa (Surguja and [[Simlipal National Park]]),<ref>{{cite journal|author=Bharos, A M K |year=1996 |title= Range extension of the Malabar Whistling Thrush, ''Myiophonus horsfieldii'' (Vigors)| journal= J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc. |volume=93|issue=2|page=295|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48603553}}</ref><ref>Panda, B., S. Pati, and B. P. Dash. "Occurrence of Malabar Whistling Thrush: Myophonus horsfieldii,(Vigors, 1831) in Similipal Biosphere Reserve of Odisha. Bird-o-soar# 05." ''Zoo's Print'' 32.11 (2017): 37.</ref> and locally in the [[Eastern Ghats]].<ref>{{cite journal|author=Ananth, G |year=1982| title= Malabar Whistling Thrush near Madanapalle|journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] | volume=22|issue=3&4|pages=10–11}}</ref> Populations are not [[bird migration|migratory]] but are known to disperse widely in winter.<ref>{{cite journal | author=Praveen, J. | year=2006 | title=Post-monsoon dispersal of Malabar Whistling Thrush ''Myiophonus horsfieldii'' (Vigors) to Chamundi Hill and Nandi Hills, Karnataka, Southern India | journal=Zoos' Print Journal | volume=21 | issue=9 | page=2411 | url=http://www.zoosprint.org/ZooPrintJournal/2006/September/2411.pdf | doi=10.11609/jott.zpj.1504.2411 | archive-date=3 June 2018 | access-date=3 January 2008 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180603204049/http://www.zoosprint.org/ZooPrintJournal/2006/September/2411.pdf }}</ref> An individual that was ringed in Mahabaleshwar in the summer of 1972 was recovered in the winter of 1976 in [[Sampaje]], Coorg.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Ambedkar, V C |year=1991| title= Long distance movement of a Malabar Whistling Thrush (''Myiophonus horsfieldii'') (Vigors) in the Western Ghats| journal= J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume=88|issue=1|page=113|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48673519}}</ref> Although historically recorded twice from [[Mount Abu]],<ref name=delacour>{{cite journal|last=Delacour|first=J|title=The whistling thrushes (Genus ''Myiophoneus'')|journal=Auk|year=1942|pages=246–254|url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v059n02/p0246-p0264.pdf|doi=10.2307/4079555|volume=59|issue=2|jstor=4079555}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last=Butler|first=EA|title=Notes on the avifauna of Mount Aboo and northern Guzerat|journal=Stray Feathers|year=1875|volume=3|issue=3|pages=437–500}}</ref> more recent surveys have not recorded the bird or suitable habitat in that location.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Sangha|first=Harkirat Singh|author2=Dhirendra Devarshi|title=Birds of Mount Abu Wildlife Sanctuary, Rajasthan, India|journal=Indian Birds|date=March–April 2006|volume=2|issue=2|pages=26–32|url=http://www.indianbirds.in/pdfs/Birds%2520of%2520Mount%2520Abu%2520Wildlife%2520Sanctuary.pdf|access-date=10 February 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110720175455/http://www.indianbirds.in/pdfs/Birds%20of%20Mount%20Abu%20Wildlife%20Sanctuary.pdf|archive-date=20 July 2011}}</ref>

==Behaviour and ecology==
They are usually seen singly or in pairs. Wherever there is suitable habitat, they are often found close to human habitation. The male sings its varied and melodious whistling song from trees during summer. They may sing for a long time around dawn but at other times of the day they often utter sharp single or two note high-pitched whistles. They were once popular as cage birds, with the ability to learn entire tunes. They bathe frequently in water usually in the mornings and evenings but also at midday during hot weather.<ref name="mccann" />

=== Food and feeding ===
[[File:Malabar_whistling_thrush_(cropped).jpg|thumb|left|Foraging on the ground]]The species is omnivorous, feeding primarily on insects, snails, worms, crabs<ref>{{cite journal|author=McCann, Charles |author-link=Charles McCann|year=1937| title= Notes on the common land crab ''Paratelphusa (Barytelphusa) guerini'' (M.) of Salsette Island|journal= J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc. |volume=39|issue=3| pages=531–542|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/47591000}}</ref> and small frogs, as well as [[Drupe|drupes]], and wind-fallen figs and berries.<ref name="hbk" /> Occasionally they have been recorded eating small snakes<ref>{{Cite web|last1=kuttettan|first1=munnar|last2=kallettumkara|first2=raphy|title=eBird checklist from Pothamedu, Munnar|url=https://ebird.org/india/checklist/S35365375|access-date=6 February 2022}}</ref> and rare records of eating longer snakes such as the Rat snake have been reported.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Sayyed|first1=Amit|last2=Gokulkrishnan|first2=G|last3=Patil|first3=Rajgopal|last4=Mahabal|first4=Anil|date=2017|title=Malabar Whistling Thrush feeding on rat snake|url=http://indianbirds.in/pdfs/IB_14_2_Abraham_CattleEgret.pdf|journal=Indian Birds|volume=14|pages=57–58}}</ref> 

=== Breeding ===
[[File:Malabar Whistling Thrush, Tamhini Pune.jpg|thumb|left|At nest]]
The birds breed from March to December and begin with the arrival of the monsoons.<ref name=mccann>{{cite journal|author=McCann, C|year=1931| title= Notes on the Whistling School Boy or Malabar Whistling Thrush (''Myiophoneus horsfieldi'', Vigors)|journal= J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume=35|issue=1|pages=202–204|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/47540170}}</ref> Courtship involves chasing flights and calling.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Navarro, SJ |year=1981|title=Observations of pair formation |journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]] |volume=21|issue=5| pages=3–7|url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW21#page/n56/mode/1up}}</ref> They usually nest in a cavity on a stream bank but will sometimes make use of nearby buildings.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Betham, R M |year=1903| title= Curious site for nesting chosen by the Malabar Whistling-Thrush ''Myiophoneus horsfieldi''|journal= J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc. |volume=14|issue=4|page= 815|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30158508}}</ref> In a study of nest-site selection in the [[Silent Valley National Park|Silent Valley]] area, a total of 21 nests were found mostly on rocks along the edge of streams and one each in a tree hole and inside an abandoned building. The nest is a cup made of moss, bamboo roots and grass, with a broad base and tapering towards the top. The base of the nest appeared to be cemented to the rock with mud. Mean nest height was 14.8 cm and depth 7.4 cm. The mean outer width and inner width were 21.5 cm and 13.1 cm respectively. Mean height from the ground was 125.8 cm.<ref name=anoop>{{cite book|author=Anoop Das, K.S.|author2=Vijayan L. |year=2003| chapter=Nest and nest site selection of Malabar Whistling Thrush in Silent Valley|title=Proc. of the 28th Conference of the Ethological Soc. of India, Mundanthurai, Tirunelveli |editor1= R. Annamalai|editor2=M,. Narayanan |editor3= Juliet Vanitharani| publisher=Dept. Zool. Sara Tucker College, Tirunelveli & Tamil Nadu Forest Dept, KMTR, Tirunelveli|pages=83–86}}</ref> Most nest sites were about 6 m from water with 60% rock cover. The nests were mostly fully concealed and nesting success was directly related to it. The birds show high site fidelity;<ref name=hbk/> occupying and nesting near their previous season's nests.<ref name=anoop/> The clutch consists of 2 to 4 [[Egg (biology)|egg]]s.<ref>{{cite book|author=Whistler, Hugh|year=1949|title=Popular Handbook of Indian Birds. Edition 4.|publisher= Gurney and Jackson, London|pages=119–120|url=https://archive.org/stream/popularhandbooko033226mbp#page/n155/mode/2up/search/horsfieldii}}</ref><ref name=ali>{{cite book|author=Ali, Salim|year=1996|title=The Book of Indian Birds. Edition 12.|publisher=BNHS & Oxford University Press|pages=285–286}}</ref> The eggs are pale salmon pink with speckling.<ref>{{cite book| pages=124–127|url=https://archive.org/stream/nestseggsofindia01hume#page/126/mode/2up|title=The nests and eggs of Indian birds. Volume 1|author=Hume, AO|year=1889|publisher=R H Porter, London|author-link=Allan Octavian Hume}}</ref> The eggs are incubated for about 16 or 17 days by both the male and female.<ref name=hbk/>
